cards.text: When debugging, you can use {{c1::conditional}} statements to print values only when specific conditions are met, reducing output clutter.

cards.question: What debugging technique is shown here?
cards.code:
if (x < 0)
    std::cerr << "ERROR: x is negative: " << x << '\n';
cards.answer: Conditional debugging - only prints when an error condition (x < 0) is detected.
cards.explanation: This is more efficient than printing every value of x.

cards.front: What's the difference between std::cout and std::cerr?
cards.back: std::cout is for normal output (buffered), std::cerr is for error messages (unbuffered, appears immediately).

cards.text: Use {{c1::std::cerr}} for debug/error output instead of std::cout because it's {{c2::unbuffered}} and outputs immediately.

cards.front: Why might std::cerr be better than std::cout for debugging?
cards.back: std::cerr is unbuffered so output appears immediately. This is critical if your program crashes - buffered std::cout output might be lost.

cards.explanation:
Redirect debug output to a file instead of console:
  • Provides a permanent record
  • Doesn't clutter console output
  • Can be analyzed later or searched
  • Useful for long-running programs

cards.text: Print statements at function {{c1::entry}} and {{c2::exit}} help you trace program {{c3::execution flow}}.

cards.question: What debugging pattern is this demonstrating?
cards.answer: Function entry/exit tracing - logs when function is called (with parameters) and when it returns (with result).
cards.explanation: This helps track program execution flow and verify function inputs/outputs.

cards.front: When should you use conditional debug output instead of unconditional output?
cards.back: When debugging loops or frequently called code where printing every iteration would create too much output. Only print when specific conditions occur.

cards.explanation:
Place debug statements at strategic locations:
  • Function entry/exit points
  • Before/after important calculations
  • Inside conditional blocks to verify which path executes
  • In loops (conditionally if many iterations)
  • After external operations (file I/O, etc.)
